dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. How can I help you today?
I can't breathe, it started suddenly about 30 minutes ago.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where you are?
It's Mary... and I am at home in [CITY], [STATE].
What is the date today?
I think it's June 7, 2019. Oh no, this is so hard to tell you I'm so short on breath.
We're checking your airway. Do you have any difficulty breathing or choking?
No, I'm just very out of breath, I can't talk without being stopped.
Thank you. Partner, clear?
Airway is clear and patent. No obstruction noted. Nasal passages open.
Mary, how would you describe your breathing? Is it fast or heavy?
It's very fast right now, like hundreds of times a minute, I can't count.
Respirations are noted to be 34, shallow. Oxygen saturation is very low at 72%.
Your oxygen is low and your breathing is very rapid. We're going to place you on additional oxygen.
Your blood pressure and heart rate will be taken now. Ma'am, do not be concerned, we're monitoring you.
Blood pressure is 148 over 66, and the heart rate is 122.
Alright. Partner, secure IV access once we get her moving onto the stretcher.
Mary, can we take a moment and talk about your medical history?
Yes, of course.
What medical conditions do you manage on a regular basis?
I have COPD, high blood pressure, GERD, anxiety, and I had pneumonia recently. I also have swelling in my legs.
Thank you. What medications are you currently on for managing these conditions?
Let me think. I take Prinivil, Prednisone, Prozac, Albuterol, Wellbutrin, and my doctor just switched me to [CITY].
Great. Any other medications like Lotrel, Prilosec, or others?
Oh yes, I also take Lotrel, Prilosec, Zithromax recently, and Remeron.
Also, she is taking [CITY], Amlodipine, Mirtazapine, and has been on potassium supplements recently.
Thank you. Partner, note that.
Are you allergic to anything?
I'm not allergic to anything major, but I do have environmental allergies.
Is there anyone in your household who can help us with more details if needed?
I am her daughter. She tried to use her inhaler about ten times already, but it hasn't helped at all.
Thank you for that. Mary, what were you doing when this started? Can you give me any idea of what led to it?
I was sitting in my chair eating lunch when I all of a sudden couldn't catch my breath. I tried to use the nebulizer but it didn't help.
She denies chest, abdominal, or back pain.
Great. No pain noted. Partner, document that.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how difficult is it to breathe for you, with 10 being the worst?
It's a 9. It feels like I'm gasping through sand.
Does the shortness of breath get worse with any specific movement or activity?
Yes, the slightest movement makes it worse.
Blood pressure is 148 over 66.
Heart rate is 122.
Respirations are 34.
Oxygen saturation is 72%.
Sinus tachycardia now, no ST elevation or STEMI seen.
Blood glucose reading is not obtainable at this time.
GCS is 15, eye=4, verbal=5, motor=6.
We'll now perform 4-lead ECG monitoring.
4-lead ECG monitoring is in place, rhythm shows sinus tachycardia with a short QT interval.
Please allow me to examine your skin and arms for any rashes, bruising, or swelling.
Let's look at your head and neck. We'll shine a light in your eyes for a quick check if it's comfortable.
Pupils are not a focus at this time, but she is fully aware of her surroundings.
We are going to give you some medicine to help your breathing. This should give you some relief.
We've administered a nebulizer treatment with Atrovent and Albuterol. She may feel some improvement in a minute.
Mary, we are going to help you with some breathing support. Will you agree to using a CPAP?
Yes, I want you to do whatever you can.
CPAP applied successfully. She reports immediate relief.
Based on the findings, we will follow protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress/COPD. We are prepared for transport and ready for reassessments.
Now her heart rate is 153, respirations 37, and her blood pressure has dropped to 123 over 66.
Her oxygen saturation is now at 69%. She remains alert. CPAP is showing good response.
12-lead EKG was taken. Findings showed Mobitz type II AV block and signs consistent with pulmonary disease-no STEMI per report.
New 4-lead EKG in place. Sinus tachycardia still present.
How are you feeling now on the breathing support? Any change in air in your chest?
It feels better a little bit, but I'm still battling. I can't speak in full sentences yet.
Did anything change in the last hour prior to the CPAP and nebulizers?
She didn't sleep well last night and had mild acid reflux this morning. She took it easy and then suddenly started struggling.
Mary, we'd like to transport you to [COMPANY_NAME], which has the best facilities for managing critical respiratory cases.
That is fine. I can prepare her for transport.
We have her on a stretcher with proper straps and CPAP secured. She is ready for transport.
Her IV is in place, and we are keeping her saturated with 12L O2. Everything is ready before the load screen.
Final confirmation on protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress. No contraindications. All procedures executed.
Final note: She is currently taking Apixaban ([CITY]) as a blood thinner, reports no pain, and no new acute findings at this time.
